When flow and oxygen are restored, instead of simple recovery, a burst of reactive oxygen species is generated, overwhelming the antioxidant defences and damaging cell membranes and structures; the restoration also triggers an inflammatory response, with activation of leucocytes and the complement and clotting systems, and calcium overload, which together injure the cells and the microvasculature, sometimes causing the no-reflow phenomenon where capillary flow is not restored. So ischaemia-reperfusion injury is the additional damage caused when blood flow is restored to ischaemic tissue, through a burst of reactive oxygen species, an inflammatory response and calcium overload, injuring cells and the microcirculation — relevant to tourniquet use, vascular surgery, transplantation and resuscitation, and a target for protective strategies.

qa.q: Summarise the local control of blood flow and its integration with the whole circulation.
qa.a: I would draw it together around the principle that the microcirculation is where the whole cardiovascular system fulfils its purpose — the exchange of oxygen, nutrients and wastes — and that its perfusion is controlled both locally, to match each tissue's need, and centrally, to serve the whole body. Locally, each tissue regulates its own blood flow by autoregulation, keeping flow constant despite pressure changes through the myogenic response, and by metabolic control, matching flow to metabolic rate through the vasodilator effect of accumulated metabolites, with the endothelium contributing through nitric oxide and other substances, and capillary recruitment expanding the exchange surface. These local mechanisms are overridden when necessary by the central, neural and hormonal control, chiefly the sympathetic nervous system, which can constrict regional beds to redistribute flow and maintain the arterial pressure for the whole body — as in haemorrhage, where flow is diverted from skin, gut and muscle to protect the heart and brain. So local control — autoregulation and metabolic and endothelial mechanisms — matches each tissue's flow to its need, integrated with and overridden by the central neural and hormonal control that serves the whole circulation; the microcirculation thus balancing local and global demands, and being where perfusion ultimately succeeds or fails in anaesthesia and critical illness.

qa.q: Thank you. An excellent and complete account. That concludes the station. Before we close, here are the take-home messages. First, The microcirculation comprises the arterioles (muscular resistance vessels controlling flow and contributing most systemic vascular resistance), the capillaries (thin-walled single-endothelial exchange vessels of vast surface area, no muscle) and the venules; built for exchange between blood and tissues with the arterioles regulating flow. Second, Capillary exchange is chiefly by diffusion — lipid-soluble substances and gases through the endothelial cells, water-soluble substances through the inter-cellular clefts, driven by concentration gradients over a vast area — with bulk flow by the Starling forces moving fluid and transcytosis for large molecules; capillary permeability varies (tight in brain, fenestrated/discontinuous elsewhere). Third, Tissues regulate their own flow via the arterioles; autoregulation (a plateau of nearly constant flow across a range of perfusion pressures) is produced by the myogenic response (smooth muscle contracting when stretched) and metabolic vasodilatation (CO2, H+, K+, adenosine, low O2), with endothelial mediators (nitric oxide, endothelin) — well developed in brain, heart and kidney. Fourth, Active hyperaemia matches increased flow to raised tissue activity; reactive hyperaemia is the flow surge after releasing an occlusion — both from metabolic vasodilatation. Systemically, vessels are controlled by sympathetic vasoconstrictor tone (alpha-adrenoceptors) setting vascular resistance and venous capacitance, and by hormones (angiotensin, catecholamines), able to override local control. Fifth, The venous system returns blood and is the major capacitance reservoir (~two-thirds of blood volume at low pressure); venous tone shifts blood to adjust cardiac filling. Clinically: maintain perfusion pressure within organs' autoregulatory range (impaired/shifted by disease and anaesthetics), manage venous capacitance (anaesthetic venodilatation causes hypotension), use vasoactive drugs acting on this system, and recognise microcirculatory failure in sepsis. Thank you. That concludes the station.
